---
title: "Autodiff (Enzyme)"
---

ferx-core uses automatic differentiation (AD) to compute exact gradients of
the FOCEI individual NLL with respect to the ETA vector. Exact gradients make
the inner L-BFGS loop faster and more reliable than finite differences,
especially as the number of random effects grows.

## Why exact gradients matter

The FOCEI inner loop calls $\nabla_\eta \text{NLL}(\eta)$ at every L-BFGS
step. For a model with $n_\eta$ random effects, central finite differences
require $2n_\eta$ model evaluations per gradient call. Reverse-mode AD
computes the full gradient in ~1–3 forward passes regardless of $n_\eta$.

Empirically (measured with `FERX_TIME_GRADIENTS=1` on 1-cpt oral and 2-cpt
infusion models): reverse-mode AD is **1.5–5× faster** per L-BFGS gradient
call than central FD across the tested range of models, even accounting for the
tape/backward overhead.

## Enzyme

ferx uses [Enzyme](https://enzyme.mit.edu) — a compiler plugin that
differentiates LLVM IR directly. It is not a symbolic or trace-based AD
library; it transforms the compiled bitcode of the forward pass into its
reverse (adjoint) at compile time.

**Build requirements:**

- `channel = "enzyme"` in `rust-toolchain.toml` (a custom Rust fork)
- `-Z autodiff=Enable` in `.cargo/config.toml` (ferx-r's build script writes this automatically)
- Only required at **compile time** — the resulting binary has the AD tape
  baked in and runs on any system

**CI / no-Enzyme builds:** set `FERX_NO_AUTODIFF=1` before building. ferx
falls back to central finite differences. All fits still work; wall time
increases ~2–5× on large $n_\eta$ models.

## Three gradient paths

The path is resolved **per subject at runtime** in
`estimation/inner_optimizer.rs::resolve_gradient_method()`:

| Path | Enum variant | When used |
|---|---|---|
| `AdSingleSnapshot` | Enzyme reverse-mode, one per-subject TV snapshot | Subject has no time-varying covariates; analytical PK model |
| `AdEventDriven` | Enzyme reverse-mode, per-event TV arrays | Subject has time-varying covariates; model is 1-cpt or 2-cpt IV (bolus/infusion) |
| `Fd` | Central finite differences | `FERX_NO_AUTODIFF=1`, ODE model, model has no `tv_fn`, or TV-cov on unsupported model |

`AdSingleSnapshot` is the common fast path. `AdEventDriven` ensures that
covariate values at each dose/observation time are correctly propagated through
the gradient when a subject's weight or other covariate changes over time.
ODE models currently have no AD path and always use FD.

```rust
// Simplified resolve_gradient_method logic
fn resolve_gradient_method(model: &CompiledModel, subject: &Subject) -> InnerGradientMethod {
    #[cfg(not(feature = "autodiff"))]
    return InnerGradientMethod::Fd;

    if model.tv_fn.is_none() { return InnerGradientMethod::Fd; }
    if subject.has_tv_covariates() {
        if supports_event_driven_ad(model.pk_model) {
            InnerGradientMethod::AdEventDriven
        } else {
            InnerGradientMethod::Fd
        }
    } else {
        InnerGradientMethod::AdSingleSnapshot
    }
}
```

The resolved path is stored in `FitResult::gradient_used` and exposed to R as
`fit$gradient_used`.

## `Dual` — forward-mode type

`ad/dual.rs` defines a `Dual` struct for forward-mode AD through the expression
evaluator and PK model equations. It is **not** the hot Enzyme path — it is
used for the model DSL expression interpreter and for contexts where Enzyme is
unavailable.

```rust
#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy)]
pub struct Dual {
    pub val: f64,
    pub deriv: f64,
}

impl Dual {
    pub fn variable(val: f64) -> Self { Self { val, deriv: 1.0 } }
    pub fn constant(val: f64) -> Self { Self { val, deriv: 0.0 } }
}
```

Operations implement the chain rule automatically:

```rust
let x = Dual::variable(2.0);   // x = 2, dx/dx = 1
let y = x.exp();               // y = e², dy/dx = e²
let z = y * Dual::constant(3.0); // z = 3e², dz/dx = 3e²
```

Supported operations: `exp`, `ln`, `sqrt`, `sin`, `cos`, `abs`, arithmetic
operators, and comparison (treats derivatives as zero for control-flow
branching — appropriate for the smooth functions in PK models).

## Building without Enzyme

```bash
# Install without Enzyme (for development, CI, Windows)
FERX_NO_AUTODIFF=1 R CMD INSTALL --no-lock .

# Or for the Rust crate directly
cargo build --no-default-features --features ci
```

With `FERX_NO_AUTODIFF=1`, the `autodiff` feature flag is disabled, the
Enzyme-gated `#[cfg(feature = "autodiff")]` blocks compile out, and
`resolve_gradient_method` always returns `Fd`. The `gradient_used` field in
`FitResult` reports `"finite_differences"`.

## Relevant source files

| File | Role |
|---|---|
| `ad/dual.rs` | `Dual<f64>` forward-mode number type and arithmetic impls |
| `ad/ad_gradients.rs` | Enzyme reverse-mode gradient entry points (`#[autodiff]`) |
| `ad/event_driven_ad.rs` | AD-compatible event-driven propagator for TV-covariate subjects |
| `ad/event_driven_ad_jac.rs` | Jacobian accumulation for Enzyme reverse mode |
| `estimation/inner_optimizer.rs` | `resolve_gradient_method()`, gradient dispatch, timing counters |
